**Pisa Sporting Club**, commonly referred to as **Pisa**, is an Italian professional [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") [club](/wiki/List_of_football_clubs_in_Italy "List of football clubs in Italy") based in [Pisa](/wiki/Pisa "Pisa"), [Tuscany](/wiki/Tuscany "Tuscany"). The club currently competes in [Serie A](/wiki/Serie_A "Serie A") in the [2025–26 season](/wiki/2025%E2%80%9326_Serie_A "2025–26 Serie A").[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)

The club was founded in 1909 as **Pisa Sporting Club** and refounded in 1994 as **Pisa Calcio** (and registered in *Eccellenza*, the regional football division in Italy), after the partial liquidation of the former because of economical troubles. Pisa was excluded again from Italian football in 2009, after failing to collect enough money to service the club's debts.[[4]](#cite_note-pisa_failed-4) In summer 2009 it was re-founded as **A.C. Pisa 1909**.

Pisa won two [Mitropa Cups](/wiki/Mitropa_Cup "Mitropa Cup"), in 1986 and 1988. They play their home matches at [Arena Garibaldi – Stadio Romeo Anconetani](/wiki/Arena_Garibaldi_%E2%80%93_Stadio_Romeo_Anconetani "Arena Garibaldi – Stadio Romeo Anconetani"), named after [Romeo Anconetani](/w/index.php?title=Romeo_Anconetani&action=edit&redlink=1 "Romeo Anconetani (page does not exist)"), the chairman who brought and led the club in Serie A during the 1980s. In 2016, Giuseppe Corrado bought the club and planned the new Pisa stadium. In January 2021, billionaire [Alexander Knaster](/wiki/Alexander_Knaster "Alexander Knaster") acquired a 75% stake in the available shares of the club.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pisa_SC&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Pisa S.C.

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pisa_SC&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Pisa S.C.")]

After promotion to [Serie B](/wiki/Serie_B "Serie B") in 1965, Pisa took three years to reach [Serie A](/wiki/Serie_A "Serie A") for the first time. Pisa was relegated on the final day of the 1968–69 season.

Spending much of the 1970s in Serie C, Pisa returned to Serie B in 1979 (by which time the club had come under the presidency of the much-loved [Romeo Anconetani](/w/index.php?title=Romeo_Anconetani&action=edit&redlink=1 "Romeo Anconetani (page does not exist)")) and were promoted to Serie A in 1982, embarking on a period of six out of nine seasons in Serie A. With Danish international [Klaus Berggreen](/wiki/Klaus_Berggreen "Klaus Berggreen") among their stars, Pisa managed a credible 11th place in the 1982–83 Serie A with 27 points and 27 goals scored and conceded in 30 games. The following season brought relegation (during which they recorded just 3 wins and 16 draws) with 15,000 fans travelling to [Milan](/wiki/A.C._Milan "A.C. Milan") for the fateful penultimate game.

Promotion followed in 1985, and the team seemed capable of staying up until losing their last three games. The cycle was repeated in 1987, only for a side containing players like [Dunga](/wiki/Dunga "Dunga") and [Paul Elliott](/wiki/Paul_Elliott_(footballer) "Paul Elliott (footballer)") to stay up. The last promotion to Serie A was achieved in 1990, and with the talents of players like [Maurizio Neri](/wiki/Maurizio_Neri "Maurizio Neri"), [Michele Padovano](/wiki/Michele_Padovano "Michele Padovano"), and [Lamberto Piovanelli](/w/index.php?title=Lamberto_Piovanelli&action=edit&redlink=1 "Lamberto Piovanelli (page does not exist)") up front and [Diego Simeone](/wiki/Diego_Simeone "Diego Simeone"), [Henrik Larsen](/wiki/Henrik_Larsen "Henrik Larsen"), and [Aldo Dolcetti](/wiki/Aldo_Dolcetti "Aldo Dolcetti") in midfield, the side started well and was briefly atop the standings, only to suffer another relegation.

Relegation brought considerable financial strains to the club, and by 1994 they had lost a relegation play-off and were condemned to [Serie C1](/wiki/Lega_Pro_Prima_Divisione "Lega Pro Prima Divisione").

### Pisa Calcio

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pisa_SC&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Pisa Calcio")]

Administration saw Pisa reformed in [Eccellenza](/wiki/Eccellenza "Eccellenza"), only to return to [Serie C2](/wiki/Lega_Pro_Seconda_Divisione "Lega Pro Seconda Divisione") in 1996 and C1 in 1999. Pisa have since worked towards attaining Serie B status, which was achieved in 2007. Their crowds have been among the better in Italy's lower divisions owing to the dedication of their fans.

In May 2002 [Maurizio Mian](/wiki/Maurizio_Mian "Maurizio Mian")'s "Gunther Reform Trust" became the owner of Pisa,[[6]](#cite_note-6) installing wealthy celebrity [German Shepherd](/wiki/German_Shepherd "German Shepherd") dog Gunther IV as honorary president. In the [2002–03 Serie C1](/wiki/2002%E2%80%9303_Serie_C1 "2002–03 Serie C1") season, Pisa reached the play-off final but were defeated in [extra-time](/wiki/Overtime_(sports) "Overtime (sports)") by [UC AlbinoLeffe](/wiki/U.C._AlbinoLeffe "U.C. AlbinoLeffe"). President Gunther would attend matches at Arena Garibaldi and [bark](/wiki/Bark_(sound) "Bark (sound)") in support of the team. On one occasion [Rival](/wiki/Football_derbies_in_Italy "Football derbies in Italy") [Livorno](/wiki/U.S._Livorno_1915 "U.S. Livorno 1915") [ultras](/wiki/Ultras "Ultras") unfurled a [banner](/wiki/Banner "Banner") bearing the legend: "Poisoned meatballs for Gunther". After two further seasons ended in mid-table finishes, Mian sold Pisa in 2005.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

In 2005–06, the team, initially thought to be a protagonist for the promotion, were in continuous struggles, and avoided relegation after playoffs in two dramatic regional derbies against [Massese](/wiki/U.S._Massese_1919 "U.S. Massese 1919"). The [2006–07](/wiki/2006%E2%80%9307_Serie_C1 "2006–07 Serie C1") season, with new boss [Piero Braglia](/wiki/Piero_Braglia "Piero Braglia"), brought Pisa back to fight for a promotion spot: the *nerazzurri* ended the regular season in third place, and eventually won the promotion playoffs by defeating [Venezia](/wiki/Venezia_F.C. "Venezia F.C.") in the semi-finals and [Monza](/wiki/A.C._Monza "A.C. Monza") in the finals.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

For the [2007–08 Serie B](/wiki/2007%E2%80%9308_Serie_B "2007–08 Serie B") campaign, the first in 13 years, [Gian Piero Ventura](/wiki/Gian_Piero_Ventura "Gian Piero Ventura") was named to replace Braglia at the helm of the *nerazzurri*. Despite initial predictions of a mid-low table place, Pisa's impressive performances brought the team to fight for a direct promotion spot, also thanks to a forward line composed by [Alessio Cerci](/wiki/Alessio_Cerci "Alessio Cerci"), [José Ignacio Castillo](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Ignacio_Castillo "José Ignacio Castillo") and [Vitali Kutuzov](/wiki/Vitali_Kutuzov "Vitali Kutuzov") which proved to be among the finest in the league. The club ended the regular season in sixth place, therefore achieving a spot to the promotion playoffs, where Pisa was later defeated by [Lecce](/wiki/U.S._Lecce "U.S. Lecce").

In 2008–09, the club was acquired by Rome entrepreneur [Luca Pomponi](/w/index.php?title=Luca_Pomponi&action=edit&redlink=1 "Luca Pomponi (page does not exist)"), who initially failed into appointing [Alessandro Costacurta](/wiki/Alessandro_Costacurta "Alessandro Costacurta") as new head coach, thus confirming Ventura as *nerazzurri* boss. The club, which was weakened by the departures of Cerci, Castillo, Kutuzov and several other players, did not manage to repeat its performances, with Ventura being ultimately sacked in March 2009, with the club in mid-table place. The appointment of [Bruno Giordano](/wiki/Bruno_Giordano "Bruno Giordano"), which was made to improve the team results, however proved to be disappointing in terms of results, as Pisa slowly lost positions in the table, and shockingly got directly relegated in the final game of the season due to an injury-time home defeat to [Brescia](/wiki/Brescia_Calcio "Brescia Calcio") which left the Tuscans in 18th place. The unexpected relegation also unveiled a number of massive financial issues which prevented the club from registering in the [Lega Pro Prima Divisione](/wiki/Lega_Pro_Prima_Divisione "Lega Pro Prima Divisione"), and in July 2009 the club was excluded by the Italian Football Federation for the second time in its history.

Home of Pisa S.C.

### A.C. Pisa 1909

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pisa_SC&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: A.C. Pisa 1909")]

Pisa has been refounded with the denomination of **A.C. Pisa 1909 S.S.D.** (in which S.S.D. is a legal suffix required by [FIGC](/wiki/Italian_Football_Federation "Italian Football Federation")) to start again from [Serie D](/wiki/Serie_D "Serie D") under new ownership.[[4]](#cite_note-pisa_failed-4) At the end of the season Pisa won Group D ([Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language"): *Girone D*) of Serie D and was promoted to [Lega Pro Seconda Divisione](/wiki/Lega_Pro_Seconda_Divisione "Lega Pro Seconda Divisione") for the 2010–11 season.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

The team was then admitted to [Lega Pro Prima Divisione](/wiki/Lega_Pro_Prima_Divisione "Lega Pro Prima Divisione") for the [2010–11 season](/wiki/2010%E2%80%9311_Lega_Pro_Prima_Divisione "2010–11 Lega Pro Prima Divisione") to fill vacancies created by a row of club exclusions in second and third tier of Italian football league system. Thus the S.S.D. legal suffix was drop and replaced by [S.r.l.](/wiki/Societ%C3%A0_a_responsabilit%C3%A0_limitata "Società a responsabilità limitata")

On 12 June 2016 Pisa gained promotion to Serie B after seven years by defeating [Maceratese](/wiki/S.S._Maceratese_1922 "S.S. Maceratese 1922") (3–1), [Pordenone](/wiki/Pordenone_Calcio "Pordenone Calcio") (3–0 on aggregate) and [Foggia](/wiki/Calcio_Foggia_1920 "Calcio Foggia 1920") in the two-legged play-off final (5–3 on aggregate),[[9]](#cite_note-9) however, the club was relegated to Serie C the following season after finishing second-last.

### Pisa Sporting Club

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pisa_SC&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Pisa Sporting Club")]

Having moved back to [Serie B](/wiki/2019%E2%80%9320_Serie_B "2019–20 Serie B") in 2019, the club changed back its name to **Pisa Sporting Club** in the summer of 2021.[[10]](#cite_note-10) Under the leadership of coach [Filippo Inzaghi](/wiki/Filippo_Inzaghi "Filippo Inzaghi"), Pisa secured promotion to Serie A by finishing as runners-up in the [2024–25 Serie B](/wiki/2024%E2%80%9325_Serie_B "2024–25 Serie B") season, marking a significant milestone in the club's resurgence and ending a 34-year absence from the top tier of Italian football.[[11]](#cite_note-11)
